blocks.content: Tuesday. Maya again. This time in the cafeteria — she was at the end of a table with her sketchbook open, alone in the specific way that means she chose it.
Jonathan wasn't there. It was just me and an empty chair.
I sat down.

blocks.speaker: Maya
blocks.content: Hey.

blocks.speaker: Larry
blocks.content: Hey. You drawing?

blocks.speaker: Maya
blocks.content: Trying to. My brain won't leave me alone today.

blocks.speaker: Larry
blocks.content: What's it doing.

blocks.speaker: Maya
blocks.content: Cataloguing every mistake I made in the last two weeks. You know how it is.

blocks.speaker: Larry
blocks.content: Yeah.

blocks.speaker: Maya
blocks.content: Does that happen to you?

blocks.speaker: Larry
blocks.content: More than I want to admit.

blocks.speaker: Maya
blocks.content: What do you do with it.
I thought about that for a second. The real answer, not the easy one.

blocks.speaker: Larry
blocks.content: I sit with it until it gets smaller. Sometimes that takes a day. Sometimes longer. But it always gets smaller eventually.

blocks.speaker: Maya
blocks.content: That's actually helpful.

blocks.speaker: Larry
blocks.content: Don't sound so surprised.

blocks.speaker: Maya
blocks.content: I'm not — I'm just —
She laughed. Not a polite one. A real one.

We talked for the rest of lunch. Not about anything big. About the sketchbook, about a show she was watching, about the math teacher who could not figure out the projector. It was easy in a way I hadn't felt in a while.
When the bell went she closed the sketchbook and didn't make anything out of it. Just: 'see you,' and walked.
That was the whole thing. And it was enough.
